content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 1102 Q Street• Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
David Kirby 
Water Quality Engineer 
Watershed Protection District 
1 790 Corte Monarca 
Camarillo,.CA 93012 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-18-220 
Dear Mr. Kirby: 
November 6, 2018 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the revolving door provisions of the 
Political Reform Act ("Act"). 1 Please note that we are only providing advice under the revolving 
door provisions of the Act and not under other prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest, 
Section 1090, or any applicable locally-imposed post-governmental employment restrictions. 
Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71 ); any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not 
the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
QUESTION 
As a local official contemplating assisting your spouse's private consulting firm in 
providing services to your former local governmental agency, the Ventura County Public Works 
Agency, how do the Act's revolving door provisions apply to you? 
CONCLUSION 
The local one-year ban is not applicable to you as former employee of the Ventura County 
Public Works Agency. However, the County of Ventura and/or your former agency may have 
adopted its own ordinance or policy restricting your activities. Hence, you should consult your 
County Counsel regarding any locally-imposed restrictions. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTOR 
File No. A-18-220 
Page No. 2 
You separated from county service on October 7, 2018. You were employed by the Ventura 
County Public Works Agency as an Engineer IV, an unelected position designated in your former 
agency's conflict-of-interest code. During your employment with the Public Works Agency, you 
were never considered a chief administrative officer. 
Your wife owns a storm-water consulting firm that operates in the same field in which you 
performed engineer duties for the Public Works Agency. Your wife's firm intends on pursuing 
future job opportunities with the Public Works Agency of providing services, such as: storm-water 
project design, storm-water sampling, technical guidance manual preparation, site inspections, and 
discretionary/ministerial permit reviews. 
You would like to assist the firm in providing these services to your former agency. Your 
name would be included within the statement of qualifications packages submitted to the Public 
Works Agency for potential work consideration in accordance with standard County contracting 
requirements. However, you would not receive an hourly wage, annual salary, be listed on any 
p~yroll documents, or receive a Form W-2 or Form 1099. 
ANALYSIS 
Public officials who leave governmental service may be subject to post-governmental 
employment provisions under the Act and certain local officials are subject to a one-year ban under 
Section 87406.3. Additionally, prior to leaving office, officials including local officials may not 
take part in decisions with a financial effect on a prospective employer under Section 87407. These 
provisions are also commonly referred to as the "revolving door" prohibitions.2 
The local "one-year ban" prohibits certain former local officials from communicating with 
their former agencies, for compensation and in representation of another person, for the purpose of 
influencing any legislative or administrative actions, including quasi-legislative and quasi-judicial 
actions, or any discretionary actions involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of 
a permit, license, grant or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property. (Section 87406.3.) 
However, Section 87406.3 's one-year prohibition, only applies to local elected officials, chief 
administrative officers of a county, city managers, or general managers or chief administrators of a 
special district who held a position with a local government agency as defined in Section 82041. 
As a local government official that was neither elected nor held a chief administrative 
position, the local one-year ban prohibition does not apply to you. However, please note that 
Section 87406.3 does not preclude a local governmental agency from adopting its own ordinance or 
policy restricting the activities of former agency officials so long as the ordinance or policy is more 
2 In response to your inquiry regarding the applicability of Section 87100, conflict of interest provisions 
relating to government decisions by a public official require a separate analysis from those relating to the post-
governmental employment restrictions. Section 87100 is inapplicable to your request for advice. Please contact the 
Commission if a situation arises where you are making, participating in making, or otherwise using your official 
position to influence a governmental decision in which you have a financial interest. Moreover, because you have 
already left governmental service, we do not address the ban on influencing prospective employment under Section 
87407. 


File No. A-18-220 
Page No. 3 
restrictive than 87406.3. You should consult your County Counsel regarding any locally-imposed 
restrictions. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
RPOC:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
By: 
Ryan P. O'Connor 
Counsel, Legal Division
